romania.title: Lumina de la Tescani: între memoria locului și curajul viitorului
romania.summary: Tabăra Internațională de Creație „Rezidența Tescani” (1–7 august 2026) unește tradiția fondată de Ilie Boca cu o nouă viziune asupra artei românești Rezidența Tescani, coordonată de artistul vizual Ovidiu Ungureanu,… Articolul Lumina de la Tescani: între memoria locului și curajul viitorului apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Conferința “Educația copilului în România: între realitate și potențial”
romania.summary: Pe 3 și 4 octombrie 2026, Bucureștiul găzduiește prima ediție a conferinței „Educația copilului în România: între realitate și potențial”, un eveniment care reunește profesori, părinți, psihologi, cercetători și lideri din… Articolul Conferința “Educația copilului în România: între realitate și potențial” apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Peace, Bro!
romania.summary: Copiii, părinții și profesorii au învățat împreună să recunoască, să prevină și să combată bullyingul, contribuind la construirea unor școli mai sigure și mai incluzive. Articolul Peace, Bro! apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: Harmony – Viitorul începe cu respect
romania.summary: Educație, colaborare și empatie pentru un mediu școlar mai sigur. Articolul Harmony – Viitorul începe cu respect apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: PWN România (Professional Women’s Network Romania) a aniversat împlinirea a 15 ani de activitate în România și deschide un nou capitol, cu un nou Consiliu Director
romania.summary: PWN România a reunit aproximativ 250 de invitați la evenimentul anual și aniversar de vară, intitulat „Garden of Growth”, și pregătește, din toamnă, relansarea programelor dedicate antreprenoriatului, mentoratului și reprezentării… Articolul PWN România (Professional Women’s Network Romania) a aniversat împlinirea a 15 ani de activitate în România și deschide un nou capitol, cu un nou Consiliu Director apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Timișoara: Investiții la Spitalul Clinic de Urgenta pentru Copii “Louis Turcanu” pentru creșterea capacității și dotarea infrastructurii spitalicești dedicate îngrijirii pacientului critic neonatal
romania.summary: Spitalul Clinic de Urgenta pentru Copii “Louis Turcanu” Timișoara anunță finalizarea proiectului cu titlul: “Șansa la viață prin creșterea capacității și dotarea infrastructurii spitalicești dedicate îngrijirii pacientului critic neonatal la… Articolul Timișoara: Investiții la Spitalul Clinic de Urgenta pentru Copii “Louis Turcanu” pentru creșterea capacității și dotarea infrastructurii spitalicești dedicate îngrijirii pacientului critic neonatal apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: „Gentle Monster”, cu Léa Seydoux și Catherine Deneuve, deschide ANONIMUL 2026
romania.summary: Cea de-a 23-a ediție a Festivalului Internațional de Film Independent ANONIMUL va fi deschisă de filmul „Gentle Monster”, un studiu psihologic intens despre încredere, memorie și destrămarea unei familii confruntate… Articolul „Gentle Monster”, cu Léa Seydoux și Catherine Deneuve, deschide ANONIMUL 2026 apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Un român susţine că nu doar Pământul e plat, ci şi burta lui appeared first on Times New Roman .

[…] The post Sportul renaşte! Naţionala de cocalari a României s-a calificat la Insula Mondială a Iubirii din 2028 appeared first on Times New Roman .

Surse din zona de securitate cibernetică spun că temutul hacker care a atacat infrastructura IT a Agenţiei Naţionale de Cadastru a reuşit să îşi treacă în proprietate o locuinţă de peste 11 metri pătraţi într-o zonă destul de umană a municipiului Cluj. „Având acces la baza de date a […] The post S-a aranjat pe viaţă! Hackerul care a spart Cadastrul şi-a trecut în proprietate o garsonieră la Cluj appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Schimbarea educației din România de jos în sus, prin pregătirea generației a 13-a de profesori-soluție pentru școli din mediul rural.
romania.summary: București, 17 iulie 2026 – Teach for Romania a dat startul Academiei de Leadership și Formare în Educație, programul intensiv prin care pregătește cea de-a 13-a generație de viitori lideri în educație – directori, profesori, învățători și educatori, care să predea, începând cu anul școlar 2026-2027, și să transforme educația de la firul ierbii, în […]

Articolul Aproape 100.000 de copii învață în clase simultane. World Vision România propune o soluție națională pentru profesorii care predau mai multor clase în aceeași oră. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas
romania.summary: Descoperă povestea Alinei și cum sprijinul oferit de Fundația Inocenți a ajutat-o să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile școlare și curajul de a reuși. The post Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

romania.title: Părinte Social
romania.summary: Părinte Social (Mamă SOS) – București și Cisnădie, Sibiu Transformă un loc de muncă într-o misiune de viață La SOS Satele Copiilor România, credem că fiecare copil are dreptul să... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

romania.title: Psiholog
romania.summary: Locație: Bacău | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

medical.title: How systemic immune regulation is 'hijacked' during tumor progression
medical.summary: The immune system plays a critical role in protecting the body from cancer by recognizing and eliminating abnormal cells before they can develop into tumors. This process relies on T cells that can recognize tumor-derived antigens presented by dendritic cells (DCs). However, tumor cells frequently evade immune surveillance and establish immune tolerance through various mechanisms, most of which are thought to occur within the tumor microenvironment.

medical.title: RNA testing uncovers 171 mutations driving MET exon 14 skipping in lung cancer
medical.summary: New research has shown that RNA-based testing can improve the detection and interpretation of clinically actionable genomic alterations that lead to MET exon 14 skipping in patients with lung cancer—important biomarkers that may be missed by DNA-only approaches. The findings from the study published in The Journal of Molecular Diagnostics are poised to improve diagnostic accuracy and help determine patients' eligibility for MET-targeted therapies.

medical.title: Parents' socioeconomic status is more important than prenatal behaviors to a future child's health, study suggests
medical.summary: Family socioeconomic position may be a more important determinant of children's health than parental behaviors such as smoking, drinking or caffeine consumption, according to a study published in PLOS Medicine by Gemma Sharp from the University of Exeter, UK, and colleagues.

medical.title: Why air travel can be so hard on the body
medical.summary: At any given time, hundreds of thousands of people are traveling by air. For many, these journeys will pass without consequence. But for some, a routine flight may trigger a health emergency.

medical.title: Study reveals stress a significant factor in women experiencing long COVID
medical.summary: Stress appears to play a role in women with young children who are diagnosed with post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection, known as Long COVID, and those who report having Long COVID symptoms. This finding, based on results from 2,500 U.S. women who completed an online survey in July 2023, is reported with related findings in an article published in Health Psychology.

medical.title: Aspirin reverses diet-driven depression-like behavior in mice
medical.summary: Depression is among the most common psychiatric disorders, estimated to affect between 280 million and 332 million people worldwide. This disorder is characterized by persistent sadness and hopelessness, low energy, a loss of interest in everyday activities and sometimes changes in appetite or sleep.

medical.title: Traveling waves in rat brains give hints about information transfer in consciousness
medical.summary: How and why we experience consciousness is a question that has long plagued philosophers and scientists alike. We have come to understand that, when awake, our brains organize neural information for perception, yet we completely lose this organization under anesthesia. Why this happens is a longstanding mystery in neuroscience, as simple changes in the activity levels of specific brain regions fail to explain this disappearance of consciousness.

medical.title: Replacing gas stoves with electric stoves greatly reduces asthma symptoms
medical.summary: More than one-third of American households rely on natural gas stoves to prepare their meals.

medical.title: Cerebral small vessel disease: New treatment possibilities for the little‑known condition affecting millions of people
medical.summary: Cerebral small vessel disease (CSVD), which notably increases the risk of stroke and cognitive disorders, is estimated to affect more than 5 million people in France alone. In the absence of a curative treatment, preventing cardiovascular risk factors remains the most effective strategy today. However, promising new therapeutic avenues are being explored.

medical.title: Brain cancer map uncovers thousands of hidden targets for personalized immunotherapies
medical.summary: Researchers from the Clinical Neuroscience Consortium (CNC), a collaborative platform jointly established by the LKS Faculty of Medicine of the University of Hong Kong (HKUMed) and Queen Mary Hospital, in collaboration with the Hong Kong Genome Institute (HKGI), have developed the most comprehensive map to date of isoform diversity in glioblastoma, the most common and aggressive form of brain cancer in adults.

science.title: Chocolate syrup-like fluid stores multiple interacting memories
science.summary: Animals and electronic devices aren't the only things with memory. Materials can retain memories of past deformations in their microscopic structure. A common example is a crease in a sheet of paper that has been folded then unfolded. Understanding this type of memory could benefit the design of materials that respond to changes in their environment in predictable ways.

science.title: There's a business case for companies to embrace sustainability, if it's pitched the right way
science.summary: Sustainable business practices are often seen as a luxury that only rich countries can afford. And these days, some of the biggest economies, including the U.S., are backtracking on those commitments.

science.title: Global map reveals mining's uneven toll on forests and biodiversity
science.summary: A new study led by Japanese researchers from Tohoku University and the National Institute for Environmental Studies (NIES) reveals that some of the minerals most essential for green technologies may have a significant negative impact on biodiversity. Using remote sensing and machine learning, the scientists produced an unprecedented global map of mined commodities.

science.title: Engineers observe quantum heat waves at room temperature
science.summary: Efficient heat management in solids is key to advancing the next generation of electronics. However, wave-like heat movement—known as phonon focusing—had been observed only at extremely low, or cryogenic, temperatures, limiting its study and practical use.

science.title: How babywearing helps establish parent‑child bonds
science.summary: From the moment they enter the world, human babies depend on others in a way that is unmatched across most species.

science.title: Simple circuit brings topological insulators closer to practical electrical measurement standards
science.summary: Researchers at the University of Würzburg have succeeded in detecting exceptionally robust electrical transport in a topological insulator. This could lead to new metrological applications. The work is published in the journal Nature Communications.

science.title: Machine learning narrows search for additional particles in the Higgs boson family
science.summary: What if the Higgs boson found in 2012 is not alone but is the only sibling we have encountered so far? Scientists at CERN discovered the particle that year, and it was a major discovery because it explained how other particles acquire mass. For a long time, scientists thought this was the final piece of the puzzle.

science.title: Universal structure of exceptional points revealed in nonlinear light‑based systems
science.summary: Exceptional points, or EPs for short, are among the phenomena of modern physics. These are special points or locations at which the properties of matter, space or time change. In a new theoretical study, researchers from the Institute for Photonic Quantum Systems (PhoQS) at Paderborn University, in collaboration with researchers from the University of Arizona, have shown that exceptional points in nonlinear systems follow a universal geometric order—something that was previously unclear. Their findings have been published in the journal Nature Communications.

science.title: Destructive crop caterpillar harbors fungi that can degrade Styrofoam
science.summary: A moth caterpillar identified by the agribusiness sector as one of the most destructive pests of soybean, cotton and corn crops may harbor fungi and bacteria in its gut that can degrade expanded polystyrene, also known as Styrofoam. In an article published in the journal BMC Microbiology, researchers from the Federal University of São Carlos (UFSCar) and São Paulo State University (UNESP) in Brazil presented the results of a study investigating the gut microbiota of Helicoverpa armigera and its role in degrading this petroleum-derived plastic.

science.title: NASA's new space telescope will tackle some of the universe's biggest mysteries
science.summary: NASA is about to launch a space observatory that could help settle some of the biggest mysteries in modern astronomy. The Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope will study how the universe began, what it is made of and how it has evolved over time.

science.title: Mars auroras reveal miniature version of Earth's magnetic engine
science.summary: NASA MAVEN (Mars Atmosphere and Volatile Evolution) mission scientists have uncovered a key puzzle piece in understanding certain types of auroras on Mars, finding that they form in a similar way to Earth–based auroras.

science.title: Spent coffee grounds reveal a route to biodiesel and other bio-based products
science.summary: Coffee grounds that usually end up being thrown away can have a second life as a raw material for producing biofuels and other high-value-added products. A study by the Universitat Rovira i Virgili (URV) has evaluated how to extract oil from coffee grounds efficiently while preserving the rest of the plant material so that it can also be used in other processes.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

World Vision România propune o soluție națională pentru profesorii care predau mai multor clase în aceeași oră. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Descoperă povestea Alinei și cum sprijinul oferit de Fundația Inocenți a ajutat-o să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile școlare și curajul de a reuși. The post Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Chocolate syrup-like fluid stores multiple interacting memories
mix_items.summary: Animals and electronic devices aren't the only things with memory. Materials can retain memories of past deformations in their microscopic structure. A common example is a crease in a sheet of paper that has been folded then unfolded. Understanding this type of memory could benefit the design of materials that respond to changes in their environment in predictable ways.

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Părinte Social (Mamă SOS) – București și Cisnădie, Sibiu Transformă un loc de muncă într-o misiune de viață La SOS Satele Copiilor România, credem că fiecare copil are dreptul să... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: Bacău | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: There's a business case for companies to embrace sustainability, if it's pitched the right way
mix_items.summary: Sustainable business practices are often seen as a luxury that only rich countries can afford. And these days, some of the biggest economies, including the U.S., are backtracking on those commitments.

mix_items.title: Global map reveals mining's uneven toll on forests and biodiversity
mix_items.summary: A new study led by Japanese researchers from Tohoku University and the National Institute for Environmental Studies (NIES) reveals that some of the minerals most essential for green technologies may have a significant negative impact on biodiversity. Using remote sensing and machine learning, the scientists produced an unprecedented global map of mined commodities.

mix_items.title: Engineers observe quantum heat waves at room temperature
mix_items.summary: Efficient heat management in solids is key to advancing the next generation of electronics. However, wave-like heat movement—known as phonon focusing—had been observed only at extremely low, or cryogenic, temperatures, limiting its study and practical use.

mix_items.title: How babywearing helps establish parent‑child bonds
mix_items.summary: From the moment they enter the world, human babies depend on others in a way that is unmatched across most species.

mix_items.title: Simple circuit brings topological insulators closer to practical electrical measurement standards
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the University of Würzburg have succeeded in detecting exceptionally robust electrical transport in a topological insulator. This could lead to new metrological applications. The work is published in the journal Nature Communications.

mix_items.title: Machine learning narrows search for additional particles in the Higgs boson family
mix_items.summary: What if the Higgs boson found in 2012 is not alone but is the only sibling we have encountered so far? Scientists at CERN discovered the particle that year, and it was a major discovery because it explained how other particles acquire mass. For a long time, scientists thought this was the final piece of the puzzle.

mix_items.title: Universal structure of exceptional points revealed in nonlinear light‑based systems
mix_items.summary: Exceptional points, or EPs for short, are among the phenomena of modern physics. These are special points or locations at which the properties of matter, space or time change. In a new theoretical study, researchers from the Institute for Photonic Quantum Systems (PhoQS) at Paderborn University, in collaboration with researchers from the University of Arizona, have shown that exceptional points in nonlinear systems follow a universal geometric order—something that was previously unclear. Their findings have been published in the journal Nature Communications.

mix_items.title: Destructive crop caterpillar harbors fungi that can degrade Styrofoam
mix_items.summary: A moth caterpillar identified by the agribusiness sector as one of the most destructive pests of soybean, cotton and corn crops may harbor fungi and bacteria in its gut that can degrade expanded polystyrene, also known as Styrofoam. In an article published in the journal BMC Microbiology, researchers from the Federal University of São Carlos (UFSCar) and São Paulo State University (UNESP) in Brazil presented the results of a study investigating the gut microbiota of Helicoverpa armigera and its role in degrading this petroleum-derived plastic.

mix_items.title: NASA's new space telescope will tackle some of the universe's biggest mysteries
mix_items.summary: NASA is about to launch a space observatory that could help settle some of the biggest mysteries in modern astronomy. The Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope will study how the universe began, what it is made of and how it has evolved over time.

mix_items.title: Mars auroras reveal miniature version of Earth's magnetic engine
mix_items.summary: NASA MAVEN (Mars Atmosphere and Volatile Evolution) mission scientists have uncovered a key puzzle piece in understanding certain types of auroras on Mars, finding that they form in a similar way to Earth–based auroras.

mix_items.title: Spent coffee grounds reveal a route to biodiesel and other bio-based products
mix_items.summary: Coffee grounds that usually end up being thrown away can have a second life as a raw material for producing biofuels and other high-value-added products. A study by the Universitat Rovira i Virgili (URV) has evaluated how to extract oil from coffee grounds efficiently while preserving the rest of the plant material so that it can also be used in other processes.

mix_items.title: How systemic immune regulation is 'hijacked' during tumor progression
mix_items.summary: The immune system plays a critical role in protecting the body from cancer by recognizing and eliminating abnormal cells before they can develop into tumors. This process relies on T cells that can recognize tumor-derived antigens presented by dendritic cells (DCs). However, tumor cells frequently evade immune surveillance and establish immune tolerance through various mechanisms, most of which are thought to occur within the tumor microenvironment.

mix_items.title: RNA testing uncovers 171 mutations driving MET exon 14 skipping in lung cancer
mix_items.summary: New research has shown that RNA-based testing can improve the detection and interpretation of clinically actionable genomic alterations that lead to MET exon 14 skipping in patients with lung cancer—important biomarkers that may be missed by DNA-only approaches. The findings from the study published in The Journal of Molecular Diagnostics are poised to improve diagnostic accuracy and help determine patients' eligibility for MET-targeted therapies.

mix_items.title: Parents' socioeconomic status is more important than prenatal behaviors to a future child's health, study suggests
mix_items.summary: Family socioeconomic position may be a more important determinant of children's health than parental behaviors such as smoking, drinking or caffeine consumption, according to a study published in PLOS Medicine by Gemma Sharp from the University of Exeter, UK, and colleagues.

mix_items.title: Why air travel can be so hard on the body
mix_items.summary: At any given time, hundreds of thousands of people are traveling by air. For many, these journeys will pass without consequence. But for some, a routine flight may trigger a health emergency.

mix_items.title: Study reveals stress a significant factor in women experiencing long COVID
mix_items.summary: Stress appears to play a role in women with young children who are diagnosed with post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection, known as Long COVID, and those who report having Long COVID symptoms. This finding, based on results from 2,500 U.S. women who completed an online survey in July 2023, is reported with related findings in an article published in Health Psychology.

mix_items.title: Aspirin reverses diet-driven depression-like behavior in mice
mix_items.summary: Depression is among the most common psychiatric disorders, estimated to affect between 280 million and 332 million people worldwide. This disorder is characterized by persistent sadness and hopelessness, low energy, a loss of interest in everyday activities and sometimes changes in appetite or sleep.

mix_items.title: Traveling waves in rat brains give hints about information transfer in consciousness
mix_items.summary: How and why we experience consciousness is a question that has long plagued philosophers and scientists alike. We have come to understand that, when awake, our brains organize neural information for perception, yet we completely lose this organization under anesthesia. Why this happens is a longstanding mystery in neuroscience, as simple changes in the activity levels of specific brain regions fail to explain this disappearance of consciousness.

mix_items.title: Replacing gas stoves with electric stoves greatly reduces asthma symptoms
mix_items.summary: More than one-third of American households rely on natural gas stoves to prepare their meals.

mix_items.title: Cerebral small vessel disease: New treatment possibilities for the little‑known condition affecting millions of people
mix_items.summary: Cerebral small vessel disease (CSVD), which notably increases the risk of stroke and cognitive disorders, is estimated to affect more than 5 million people in France alone. In the absence of a curative treatment, preventing cardiovascular risk factors remains the most effective strategy today. However, promising new therapeutic avenues are being explored.

mix_items.title: Brain cancer map uncovers thousands of hidden targets for personalized immunotherapies
mix_items.summary: Researchers from the Clinical Neuroscience Consortium (CNC), a collaborative platform jointly established by the LKS Faculty of Medicine of the University of Hong Kong (HKUMed) and Queen Mary Hospital, in collaboration with the Hong Kong Genome Institute (HKGI), have developed the most comprehensive map to date of isoform diversity in glioblastoma, the most common and aggressive form of brain cancer in adults.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Azi sunt foarte zen. N-am energie nici sa ma stresez.

satire_ro.title: S-a molipsit? De când s-a pupat cu un urmărit internaţional, Ciolacu dă şi el semne de urmărire internaţională
